intro.kicker: Topic 4 of 10 • Chapter: Newton's Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Newton's First Law states: A body continues in its state of rest or uniform motion in a straight line unless acted upon by an external force. It gives the qualitative definition of force and establishes the concept of inertia. Mathematically: acceleration = 0 if and only if net force = 0. It was called the Law of Inertia by Galileo. High-yield for NEET conceptual questions on pseudo-force and inertial frames.

expectedQuestions.description: Conceptual MCQs on the law statement, its relation to inertia, and applications including inertial frames. Often combined with Newton's Second Law in NEET.

timeRequired.description: Cover the law statement, Galileo's connection, qualitative force definition, and inertia subtypes. Key: link to inertia of rest/motion/direction.

difficulty.description: Statement and application are straightforward. Traps involve confusing 'zero velocity' with 'zero net force' and applying Newton's First Law in accelerating frames.

nriUsaGap.description: Newton's First Law is universal. US AP Physics covers inertial frames but may not emphasise Galileo's original formulation or the 'qualitative definition of force' phrasing from NCERT.

weightage.sectionTitle: Newton's First Law — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Law statement appears as direct NEET question — memorise NCERT wording precisely.
Connection between zero net force and zero acceleration is tested conceptually.
Galileo's Law of Inertia as the forerunner to Newton's First Law is exam context.

strategySteps.title: Memorise the exact law statement
strategySteps.description: NEET directly quotes law statements. Use: 'A body continues to be in its state of rest or uniform motion along a straight line unless acted upon by external force.'

strategySteps.title: Understand the qualitative vs quantitative distinction
strategySteps.description: First Law gives qualitative definition of force (force causes change in motion). Second Law gives quantitative (F = ma). Know which law does what.

strategySteps.title: Link to inertia subtypes
strategySteps.description: Newton's First Law is the Law of Inertia. All three inertia types (rest, motion, direction) are directly governed by this law.

cards.title: Newton's First Law — Complete Notes
cards.description: Law statement, Galileo connection, qualitative force definition, inertia types, and NEET MCQ strategy.

cards.title: Quick Fact Sheet
cards.description: One-page summary of Newton's First Law statement, traps, and distinction from Second Law.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Newton's First Law of Motion

cards.title: The Law Statement
cards.coreRevision: A body continues to be in its state of rest or of uniform motion along a straight line, unless it is acted upon by some external force to change the state.
points: No net force → no acceleration (velocity remains constant)
Also called: Law of Inertia (Galileo named it)
cards.example: A hockey puck sliding on frictionless ice continues indefinitely — no net force acts on it. Add friction and it decelerates due to the friction force.

cards.title: Mathematical Statement
cards.coreRevision: If net force F = 0, then acceleration a = 0, meaning velocity stays constant (which includes being at rest: v = 0, constant).
points: Body can be at rest (v = 0) or moving (v ≠ 0, constant) when F = 0
NEET trap: 'constant velocity' includes zero velocity
cards.example: A body moving at 10 m/s in deep space (no friction, no gravity) requires zero force to maintain that speed — Newton's 1st Law.

cards.title: Galileo's Law of Inertia
cards.coreRevision: Newton's First Law was originally called the Law of Inertia by Galileo. It corrected Aristotle's fallacy that force is needed to maintain motion.
points: Aristotle (wrong): Force needed to maintain motion
Galileo (correct): Force needed only to change motion
Newton formalised Galileo's insight into the First Law
cards.example: Aristotle observed that a cart stops when pushed — but this is due to friction, not because motion requires sustained force. Galileo's inclined plane experiments revealed this error.

curriculum.hintText: Key differences between CBSE/NEET and US AP Physics focus areas for Newton's First Law.

gaps.title: Galileo's Precursor and Historical Context
gaps.description: US AP Physics focuses on applying Newton's Laws; NEET also tests the historical lineage (Galileo's Law of Inertia).
bullets: Know that Newton's 1st Law was called the Law of Inertia by Galileo
Aristotle's fallacy (force needed for uniform motion) corrected by Galileo — exam-tested

gaps.title: Qualitative vs Quantitative Force Definition
gaps.description: NEET explicitly tests which law gives the 'qualitative' vs 'quantitative' definition of force.
bullets: First Law: qualitative — force is what causes change in state of motion
Second Law: quantitative — F = ma gives a measurable relationship

questions.question: Newton's first law of motion gives the definition of:
questions.explanation: Newton's First Law gives the qualitative definition of force — force is that external agent which changes or tends to change the state of rest or uniform motion. The Second Law (F = ma) gives the quantitative definition.

questions.question: A body is said to be in equilibrium when:

options.text: Net force on it is zero
Its acceleration is constant but non-zero
It is moving in a curved path

questions.explanation: By Newton's First Law, a body remains in equilibrium (constant state of rest or uniform motion) when the net external force is zero. Zero velocity is a special case of equilibrium, but equilibrium includes uniform motion too.

questions.question: Newton's First Law was originally called the Law of Inertia by:
If no net external force acts on a body in motion at 20 m/s, the body will:
Newton's First Law of Motion establishes the concept of:
Which of Newton's Laws gives the qualitative definition of force?

items.title: Memorise the exact law statement
items.description: Body continues in state of rest or uniform motion in straight line unless external force acts.

items.title: First Law = qualitative definition of force
items.description: Distinguish from Second Law which gives quantitative definition (F = ma).

items.title: Galileo named it Law of Inertia
items.description: Historical context tested in NEET — Galileo corrected Aristotle's fallacy.

items.title: F_net = 0 ↔ a = 0
items.description: Zero net force means zero acceleration means constant velocity (not necessarily zero velocity).

items.title: Inertia types are governed by this law
items.description: All three inertia types (rest, motion, direction) are manifestations of Newton's First Law.

items.question: Does Newton's First Law mean a body at rest will always remain at rest?
items.answer: Only if no net external force acts. If a net force acts, the body will accelerate. Newton's First Law describes the state ONLY in the absence of net force.

items.question: Can Newton's First Law be derived from Newton's Second Law?
items.answer: Yes. If F = 0 in the second law (F = ma), then a = 0, meaning the velocity is constant — which is exactly the statement of the First Law. This is why the Second Law is considered the most fundamental of the three.

items.question: What is the difference between Newton's First Law and the Law of Conservation of Momentum?
items.answer: Newton's First Law applies to a single body in the absence of net force (velocity stays constant). Conservation of momentum applies to a system of bodies — internal forces may act but if net external force is zero, total momentum is conserved. They share the same root condition: zero net external force.

grounding_summary: Page 189 states Newton's First Law: a body continues in its state of rest or uniform motion along a straight line unless acted upon by external force. It also notes that zero net force means zero acceleration, and that the first law gives the qualitative definition of force.
Page 189 states Newton's First Law was called the Law of Inertia by Galileo, and that inertia has three types: rest, motion, and direction.
Page 190 details the three inertia types (rest, motion, direction) with real-life examples — all governed by Newton's First Law.
grounding_excerpt: A body continue to be in its state of rest or of uniform motion along a straight line, unless it is acted upon by some external force to change the state.
Newton's first law gives the qualitative definition of force.
Newton's first law was called the law of inertia by Galileo.
